coredata.dc:title: Dual-spot ratiometric microfluidic paper-based analytical device for accuracy and precision improvement
coredata.dc:creator: Al-Jaf, Sabah H.
Omer, Khalid M.
coredata.dc:description: Instrumental and environmental fluctuations are common sources of error in smartphone-based optical detection, significantly affecting the accuracy of analytical measurements. In this regard, spotting the sample and reference simultaneously and in close proximity compensates for the fluctuations. This “dual-spot” design is similar to the double-beam technique used in spectrophotometry, which reduces fluctuations in the results. The underlying hypothesis is that any instrumental and/or environmental factors influencing the color intensity in the detection zones will similarly impact the color intensity in the control zone under the same conditions. To test our design, a ratiometric microfluidic paper-based analytical device (μPAD), functionalized with a mixture of green-emissive carbon dots (CDs) and red-emissive ethidium bromide, was developed for the selective detection of ascorbic acid (AA). The green emission of the CDs is quenched by both AA and Fe3+; NaF was thus loaded onto the 3D connector as a masking agent to remove the interference effect of the Fe3+ ions. The color variations were monitored under a UV lamp, using a smartphone to capture the images, and the RGB intensities were processed using the Color Grab application. The proposed double-spot method greatly enhanced the analytical precision and accuracy of the device. A linear working range from 0 to 125 μM was obtained, and the limit of detection was 2.71 μM. The μPAD was successfully used for the detection of AA in human serum, with recoveries from 87.27 to 98.52 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microfluidic paper-based device
Ratiometry
3D connector
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of the CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research of Kurdistan Regional Government

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Sabah H. Al-Jaf gratefully acknowledges the Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research of Kurdistan Regional Government as well as University of Garmian for their permission and accepting his Study Leave.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrations of (A) the procedure for the fabrication of the ratiometric μPAD proposed herein, using a LaserJet printing approach; and (B) the sequence of steps in performing the measurements using this device.
(A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of AA, and excitation and fluorescence spectra of the CDs. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of AA, CDs, and g-CDs-AA.

Schematic illustration of the RGB fluctuations in the case of using the PAD (A) without the reference zone and with different camera positions; (B) with the reference zone and with different camera positions; (C) without the reference zone, with the same camera positions, and with different smartphones; and (D) with the reference zone, with the same camera positions, and with different smartphones. (E) Photographs of the device taken at seven camera positions (P1–P7). (F) Intensity of red color in the presence (R/R°) and absence (R/R°P1). (G) Intensity of green color in the presence (G/G°) and absence (G/G°P1). (H) Intensity of blue color in the presence (B/B°) and absence (B/B°P1).

(A) Color tonality of the CD–ethidium bromide mixture in the presence of different AA concentrations (0–125 μM) in a phosphate buffer at pH 7.4. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs in the presence of different AA concentrations (0–180 μM) in a phosphate buffer at pH 7.4. (C) Visual detection on the paper-based device in the presence of different AA concentrations (photographs taken under a 365 nm UV lamp). Linear correlation between the intensity of green and blue color variations and the AA concentration in the range of 0–125 μM (D) without using a separate blank correction and (E) using a separate blank correction for each standard and sample. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three replicate measurements.

(A) Selectivity of the probe towards AA (solution). Selectivity of the paper-based device in the presence of (B) cations, (C) amino acids, and (D) biomolecules. (E) Effect of the concentration of NaF as a masking agent on the color change in the detection zone upon the addition of Fe3+ to the sampling zone. (F) Optimisation of NaF concentration for masking Fe3+.
Comparison of AA detection herein with that in previous literature reports using paper-based analytical devices.
Recovery tests in real serum samples (n = 3) for the detection of spiked AA using the proposed method.

head.ce:title: Dual-spot ratiometric microfluidic paper-based analytical device for accuracy and precision improvement

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani, 46002, Sulaimani City, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani, 46002 Sulaimani City, Kurdistan region, Iraq

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Garmian, Darbandikhan Road, 46021, Kalar City, Sulaimaniyah Province, Kurdistan of Iraq, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Garmian, Darbandikhan Road, 46021, Kalar City-Sulaimaniyah Province, Kurdistan of Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Instrumental and environmental fluctuations are common sources of error in smartphone-based optical detection, significantly affecting the accuracy of analytical measurements. In this regard, spotting the sample and reference simultaneously and in close proximity compensates for the fluctuations. This “dual-spot” design is similar to the double-beam technique used in spectrophotometry, which reduces fluctuations in the results. The underlying hypothesis is that any instrumental and/or environmental factors influencing the color intensity in the detection zones will similarly impact the color intensity in the control zone under the same conditions. To test our design, a ratiometric microfluidic paper-based analytical device (μPAD), functionalized with a mixture of green-emissive carbon dots (CDs) and red-emissive ethidium bromide, was developed for the selective detection of ascorbic acid (AA). The green emission of the CDs is quenched by both AA and Fe3+; NaF was thus loaded onto the 3D connector as a masking agent to remove the interference effect of the Fe3+ ions. The color variations were monitored under a UV lamp, using a smartphone to capture the images, and the RGB intensities were processed using the Color Grab application. The proposed double-spot method greatly enhanced the analytical precision and accuracy of the device. A linear working range from 0 to 125 μM was obtained, and the limit of detection was 2.71 μM. The μPAD was successfully used for the detection of AA in human serum, with recoveries from 87.27 to 98.52 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ratiometric assays, where the quantification of targets depends on the ratio of two signals, have attracted significant attention in recent years. This technique can effectively reduce instrumental or environmental effects and offers increased analytical precision compared to conventional assays based on a single signal, especially in the case of low target concentrations or complex biological systems [1]. Ratiometric measurement is based on the self-calibration of signal intensity via the recording of two or more analyte-induced signal fluctuations, where one signal can act as a reference for normalizing the other [2]. However, in paper-based techniques, most of the optical-based ratiometric designs use one reference spot (control zone) to establish a ratio between the sampling zone and the original control or reference intensity, ignoring any environmental and/or instrumental variations during the recording of the digital photographs. Slight variations in, for example, the camera angle from run to run and the lighting of the box might have a significant effect on the measurements [3]. Thus, compensating for such variations is notably important to improve the signal-to-noise ratio, the precision and accuracy of the measurements, and the stability of the results. This is analogous to the case of a double-beam spectrophotometer, which uses two separate light paths—one for the sample and the other for the reference. By simultaneously measuring the sample and reference signals, any fluctuations or drifts in the light source, detector, or optical components can be automatically corrected, leading to the improved accuracy and reliability of the measurements. Thus, one can consider a dual-spot ratiometric design to achieve the same advantages offered by a double-beam spectrophotometer.

Paper is a cost-effective and widely available material and is becoming an increasingly popular tool in chemical and biological analyses [4]. Microfluidic paper-based analytical devices (μPADs), as first introduced by Whiteside in 2007, have evolved into a platform suitable for use in resource-limited and on-site settings due to their ease of use, portability, and low costs [5]. Paper-based devices can be fabricated and functionalized quickly and easily, making them a popular choice for various analyses worldwide. In the past, paper-based assays were conducted using two- and three-dimensional (2D and 3D) devices with multiple detection zones, though this approach presented some limitations [6]. The addition of probes to the detection zones often resulted in them flowing outside the device and entering the fluid channel, leading to experimental errors in the visual quantitative analyses [7,8].

Ascorbic acid (AA), also known as vitamin C, is a water-soluble vitamin known for its reductive characteristics; it is widely found in fruits, vegetables, and animal viscera [9] and is commonly used as an antioxidant in food, beverages, animal feed, pharmaceutical formulations, and cosmetics [10]. Furthermore, it plays a key role in several other processes, such as amino acid metabolism and ion absorption, and also acts as a cofactor in enzymatic reactions and contributes to collagen synthesis and immune system enhancement [11]. Abnormal levels of AA are closely related to many diseases, including scurvy, mental illness, diarrhea, cancer, and AIDS [12]. Extensive studies have shown that insufficient intake leads to scurvy, gingival bleeding, skin petechiae, and so forth, while excessive intake results in urinary stones and stomach convulsions [13,14]. Therefore, the development of a simple, reliable, and rapid method for AA determination with high sensitivity and selectivity is important in health monitoring and disease diagnosis.

To date, several methods have been developed for the sensitive and specific determination of AA, including electrochemical techniques [15], high-performance liquid chromatography [16], fluorescence detection [17,18], colourimetry [19,20], titrimetry [21], chemiluminescence detection [22], electrochemiluminescence sensing [23], capillary electrophoresis [24], and the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [25]. However, most of these techniques suffer from obstacles such as the need for sophisticated instruments, complex pretreatments, skilled personnel, or costly biological reagents. Therefore, developing an accurate, simple, low-cost, and precise sensing platform to overcome the aforementioned limitations is highly desirable in bioanalysis.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new class of carbon nanomaterials exhibiting fluorescence properties, have triggered extensive studies since their discovery in 2004 [26]. The fluorescence of CDs efficiently responds to the presence of analytes and has been exploited in sensing applications [27]. Furthermore, CDs have attracted significant interest by virtue of their distinctive superiority in terms of their excellent water solubility, high photostability against blinking and photobleaching, tunable excitation and emission spectra, robust chemical inertness, excellent biocompatibility, and low toxicity [28,29]. These properties have contributed to their wide use in bioimaging [30], biosensing [31], medical diagnosis [32], photodynamic therapy [33], catalysis [34], and photovoltaic applications [35]. In terms of image capturing, one of the major factors limiting the use of smartphone cameras as an analytical instrument is that the obtained signal might be affected by parameters such as the camera position, lighting conditions, and the type and model of the smartphone. Researchers have made some efforts to overcome this problem; for example, Mathaweesansurn et al. [36] developed a smartphone-based analyser and simultaneously captured an image of the test solution on the plastic plate and the reference test paper with a single shot in an attempt to reduce signal fluctuations arising from differences in illumination. Other research groups have also developed tree-like paper strips on which all sample and standard detection zones are located, aiding in the construction of the calibration curve using a single device [37,38]; however, these devices cannot completely eliminate the above-mentioned errors since their size is slightly large, and, hence, the camera focuses differently on the various detection zones due to its position. Thus, developing a design where all factors, such as the camera position and the illumination, can be controlled and corrected is important.

In the present work, we designed a ratiometric μPAD (R-μPAD) functionalized with a mixture of synthesized green-emissive CDs and ethidium bromide as dynamic and reference probes, respectively, for the selective detection of AA. A dual-spot design was implemented, where a control zone was used as a reference signal and applied separately from the detection and sampling zones. A 3D multifunctional spacer was introduced between the sampling and detection zones to serve as a masking zone (for the removal of possible interferences) and to block the dispersion of reagents from the latter towards the former, resulting in improved detection resolution. The novel design was used for the quantification of AA in serum samples. Fig. 1 depicts the dual-spot design combined with the 3D spacer to improve the figures of merit of the analysis.

All materials and reagents in this study were of analytical grade and were used directly without further purification. Rhodamine B (97 %) and ethidium bromide (95 %) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (Germany); ascorbic acid, citric acid, glucose, glutathione, creatinine, uric acid, urea, thiourea, cysteine, sodium hydroxide, ferric chloride, ferrous sulfate, sodium chloride, magnesium chloride, copper chloride, mercury chloride, and lead nitrate from Biochem Chemopharma Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Cosne Sur Loir, France); and tyrosine, proline, alanine, histidine, valine, arginine, mannose, theobromine, and lysine from Carl Roth GmbH + Co. KG (Karlsruhe, Germany). Deionised water was used for solution preparation in all experiments, and ultrapure water was obtained via a Millipore system (18.2 MΩ cm). The real serum samples were obtained from Life Private Hospital in Kalar, Sulaymaniyah (Iraq). Standard stock solutions (1000 mg L−1) of all reagents were prepared by dissolving 0.1 g of the solid powder in 100.00 mL of water. The prepared stock solutions were stored in a refrigerator (4 °C), and working standard solutions were freshly prepared via appropriate dilutions of the stock solutions with water.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected using a Tecnai G2 20 microscope (Tecnai, FEI, USA). UV–Vis spectra were recorded using a T80 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (PG, United Kingdom), and an RF–5301 P C (Shimadzu, Japan) was used for fluorescence analysis. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded with an attenuated total reflectance IRAffinity-1S FTIR spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan), and Raman spectra were recorded using a Thermo Scientific DXR Raman microscope. The amorphous nature of the CDs was measured via X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a PANalytical X'Pert X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Kα irradiation (λ = 1.54 Å) at 40 kV and 30 mA. A LaserJet printer (HP, M203) was used for the fabrication of hydrophilic zones within the hydrophobic surroundings of the μPAD.

The CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal process using rhodamine B as a precursor. Briefly, 0.1 g rhodamine B was first dissolved in 15 mL of 0.1 M NaOH and sonicated for 10 min. Subsequently, the homogeneous mixture was transferred into a 25-mL Teflon-lined autoclave chamber and heated to 200 °C for 12 h. Allowing the mixture to cool to room temperature yielded a green to brown solution; this was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove large and small suspended particles and later filtered through 0.22-μm microporous membranes to remove larger particles. Finally, the solution was dialyzed using a dialysis bag (500 Da) against double-distilled water for 24 h to remove very small particles. The purified CD solution was then vacuum dried into a powder, and the as-synthesized CDs were stored at 4 °C for further use.

The μPAD was designed using Microsoft PowerPoint and printed on Whatman No. 1 filter paper. The device consisted of two control and two detection zones, circular in shape and 6 mm in diameter, and a circular sampling zone connected to a flow channel (2 × 20 mm), which was separated from the detection zones, as shown in Fig. 1A. A hydrophobic spacer 2 × 3 mm in size was designed between the flow channel and the detection zones, and a 3D connector with the same dimensions was also developed. After printing the designed pattern onto the filter paper using a LaserJet printer (HP, M203), the paper was heated at 180 °C for 2 h in an oven and then cooled to room temperature to ensure that the ink had completely penetrated its pores (Fig. 1A).

The fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) of the prepared CDs was measured according to an established procedure by comparing the fluorescence intensities and absorption values of the CD samples with those of rhodamine B in water (QY = 0.31) as a reference. The quantum yield of the g-CDs in water was calculated according to Equation (1) [39]:
(1)
Փ CDs
=
Փ rhB
.
I CDs
I rhB
.
A rhB
A CDs
.
η CDs
η rhB
,
where Փ is the FLQY; I is the integrated fluorescence emission intensity; A denotes the absorbance, measured at 470 nm; and η is the refractive index of the solution in distilled water.

A 10.0 μL aliquot of a mixture of the CDs (5 mg mL−1) and ethidium bromide was spotted onto each of the detection and control zones and dried at room temperature for 5 min. Thereafter, the device was folded to connect the flow channel from the sampling zone to the detection zone. Next, 30.0 μL of a standard or sample solution was added to the sampling zone, flowing into the flow channel towards the detection zone; here, the AA present in the sample or standard quenched the fluorescence of the CDs, leading to a color change in the detection zone from green to red. After drying the detection zone, the images were recorded under constant lighting (in a dark box) and smartphone camera settings.

Images of the circles at the center of the detection zones were captured using a smartphone camera (Huawei, nova 3e, 2018), each at a fixed distance and under the same illumination in a dark box. The entire area of each zone was averaged after splitting the image into its red, green, and blue (RGB) components using the Color Grab application (version 3.9.2), with the intensity values of each color component ranging from 0 to 255. The green and blue channels were selected for the analysis since they showed the best correlation with the AA concentration.

Human serum samples were acquired from Kalar Public Hospital in Kurdistan, Iraq, after obtaining ethical approval (GRCEC111). These were pretreated to remove impurities prior to analysing their AA content. After first mixing equal volumes of ethanol and the serum samples, the resulting solution was heated to 95 °C for 15 min and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove proteins (deproteinization). The supernatant was stored at −20 °C for future experiments. The samples were then spiked with a series of different AA concentrations, which were detected by the proposed method described earlier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and structure of the CDs were characterized via TEM, FTIR spectroscopy, XRD, Raman spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The TEM image depicted in Fig. S1A indicates that the CD particles are almost spherical, with a significantly narrow range of diameters (1–8 nm) and an average particle size of 2 nm.

FTIR spectra were recorded to confirm the presence of various functional groups in the CDs (Fig. S1B). The characteristic broad band centered at around 3541 cm−1 could provide evidence for the presence of oxygen and amino functional groups on the CD surface [40]. The peaks at 2978, 1408, and 2361 cm−1 are assigned to C–H, CC, and C–C stretching vibrations, respectively. Furthermore, the peak at 1076 cm−1 can be attributed to C–O vibrations [41,42]. Fig. S1C depicts the XRD pattern of the synthesized CDs; the peak at 2θ = 35.6 ° is assigned to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [43].

Raman spectroscopy is one of the most important techniques for characterizing carbon-based materials. Fig. S1D displays the Raman spectrum for the synthesized CDs, with two dissimilar bands at 1375 and 1651 cm−1; these indicate the presence of D and G bands in the CDs, corresponding to sp3 defects and sp2 carbons, respectively [44]. The Raman spectroscopic results notably confirm minimal surface defects and a moderately graphitic structure [45].

Further, XPS measurements were carried out to explore the bonding, surface state, and chemical composition of the prepared CDs. Fig. S1E depicts a wide XPS full survey spectrum with the binding energy of C1s, N1s, and O1s at 284.53, 399.58, and 531.5 eV, respectively, revealing that carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen are the main elements of the as-synthesized CDs. In addition, the high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum in Fig. S1F exhibits peaks at 284.46 eV (C–H), 285.08 eV (C–C/CC), and 288.43 eV (C–N/O–CO) [46]. The N1s spectrum shows one peak at 399.58 eV (C–N–C), as presented in Fig. S1G [47], and the high-resolution O1s spectrum in Fig. S1H displays two peaks, at 530.02 eV (CO) and 531.5 eV (C–O–C) [45]. These results confirm the presence of hydroxyl, amino, and carbonyl functional groups on the CD surface, in agreement with the FTIR results.

To investigate the optical properties of the CDs, UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of aqueous solutions of the CDs were recorded. UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy is typically used to evaluate the electronic transitions in CDs. The absorption spectrum of the CDs exhibits a distinct band at approximately 470 nm (Fig. S2A), which can be assigned to π-π* (conjugated CC) transitions from the carbon core [48]; the spectral results also indicate π*-n (C–N) transitions from the surface/molecular section, resulting from strong fluorescence emission [49]. Unlike most previously reported CDs [50–52], the CDs herein exhibit an excitation-independent property with the emission maximum at 513 nm (Fig. S2B) under a 493 nm excitation wavelength. This implies that in the CDs, only one type of luminescence center exists [48]. The CDs exhibit green emission with a high quantum yield (38 %), using rhodamine B as the standard, demonstrating their good fluorescence properties.

The stability of the as-prepared CDs was investigated under various conditions. To explore the influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs, different buffer solutions (pH 3–12) were prepared, and the fluorescence intensities at each pH were recorded at 513 nm. As shown in Fig. S2C, the fluorescence intensity is slightly lower in excessively acidic (pH 3–4) or basic (pH 11–12) media as compared to media with a milder pH (pH 6–10), indicating that the CDs exhibit good stability in neutral or milder pH conditions. Further, the as-prepared CDs were stored for over 15 weeks, and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at different intervals. Fig. S2D shows that after a storage period of more than 3 months, no significant changes in intensity occur, indicating well-dispersed CDs with no aggregation or photochemical reactions affecting their structure and optical properties.

Generally, the inner filter effect (IFE), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), dynamic quenching (DQ), and static quenching (SQ) could lead to the fluorescence quenching of fluorescent materials [53–55]. To elucidate the quenching mechanism of the CDs due to AA, corresponding experiments were performed to investigate each of these effects. In this regard, the UV–Vis spectra of AA and the fluorescence spectra of the CDs were first analysed. As shown in Fig. 2 A, AA exhibits a sharp absorption band centered at around 275 nm, while the CDs show a broad excitation wavelength at 493 nm with an emission wavelength centered at 513 nm. The noticeably nonoverlapping signals between the CD excitation and AA absorption spectra indicate that AA cannot shield the excitation light of the CDs, thus eliminating the possibility of the IFE. By considering the UV–Vis spectra of the CDs before and after the addition of AA, as shown in Fig. 2B, one can note the appearance of a new peak centered at around 320 nm. These results reveal that the quenching mechanism is attributed to SQ, where AA interacts with functional groups present on the surface of the CDs to form nonfluorescent complexes.

The utilization of RGB-based analysis with cellphones, cameras, and/or scanners in paper-based devices is hindered by a notable limitation: the devices are highly sensitive to slight alterations in background elements, lighting conditions, camera positioning, and even system settings. To address these challenges, we developed a device that incorporates separate control zones (reference zones) for the individual detection areas; this design enables the correction of any fluctuations observed in the output signals of the detection zones (Fig. 3 A). The main hypothesis is that any external factor affecting the color intensity in the detection zones similarly impacts that in the control zones. By calculating the ratio of RGB values between both zones, any variations can be effectively eliminated, as shown in Fig. 3B. To validate this hypothesis, we conducted two scenarios to test the device: one employing conventional ratiometry (with one control zone), and one involving dual-spot ratiometry (the proposed design).

First Scenario: In the conventional design, a single control zone was used for all sample detection. Images of the detection zone were captured using the same smartphone and identical lighting conditions and camera settings. However, the camera positions for each test were varied, ranging from P1 to P4, as shown in Fig. 3B–E. The RGB values of the detection zone at the different camera positions were divided by their corresponding values at the first camera position (P1). The results reveal fluctuations in the values of R/R°P1, G/G°P1, and B/B°P1 at the different camera positions, where R°, G°, and B° refer to the intensity of the red, green, and blue channels at the control position. Similar results were obtained when using the same lighting conditions and camera positions but different smartphones (S1–S4), as depicted in Fig. 3C.

Second Scenario: In the dual-spot design, a control zone was present for each detection zone (Fig. 3A). Images of each detection zone and its control zone were captured using the same smartphone and identical lighting conditions and camera settings. However, we varied the camera position for each test, ranging from P1 to P4, as shown in Fig. 3B–E. Subsequently, to evaluate the effect of the presence of the control channels along with the individual detection zones, the RGB values of each detection zone were divided by the corresponding values of its control zone at each camera position. Fig. 3B–E shows that the fluctuations in the R/R°, G/G°, and B/B° values at the different camera positions are significantly minimized. Thus, the various fluctuations arising from different lighting conditions and camera positions can be compensated for, and any resulting small errors can be corrected. Fluctuations are also minimized when employing the same lighting conditions and camera positions but different smartphones (S1–S4), as shown in Fig. 3D.

The feasibility of using the prepared CDs for AA detection in sensing applications was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 B, with the addition of AA, the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared CDs decreases significantly. For the preparation of the ratiometric probe, the CDs, serving as a response probe, and ethidium bromide, used as a reference probe, were mixed to yield a solution with green emission due to the higher CD concentration. Then, the addition of different AA concentrations gradually turned the solution color to red due to the quenching of the green emission of the CDs by AA, as shown in Fig. 4A. Ethidium bromide solution was used as the reference probe due to its high fluorescence intensity and the fact that its fluorescence is not affected by AA or the tested matrices that might be present in the sample. In addition, its red fluorescence in the mixture with the green-emissive CDs provides maximum color tonality for the ratiometric probe design. The fabricated paper-based device was functionalized by adding an appropriate amount of the reagent probes to each of the detection and control zones, and NaF was applied as a masking agent to the 3D connector to mask Fe3+. After drying at room temperature for 5 min, the device was folded to connect the detection zones and the channel from the sampling zone via the 3D connector. The samples or standards were then added to the sampling zone, flowing through the flow channel towards the detection zone where they quenched the fluorescence of the CDs, marked by the color change of the solution from green to red under UV light. A photograph of the device was subsequently taken using a smartphone camera, and the colors of the detection and control zones were processed by the Color Grab application. As shown in Fig. 4C, the color of the detection zones changed from green to red by increasing the concentration of AA. By plotting the ratios of the blue and green channels for the detection and control zones against the AA concentration, a linear relation in the range of 0–125 μM (R2 = 0.997) was obtained, as shown in Fig. 4E, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 2.71 μM. The results obtained via the proposed μPAD design are comparable to those previously reported in the literature for the detection of AA using paper-based analytical devices, as presented in Table 1 .

To evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method, several potentially interfering molecules and metals commonly present in serum samples were employed. Possible interferences, such as citric acid, glucose, glutathione, creatinine, uric acid, urea, thiourea, cysteine, tyrosine, proline, alanine, histidine, valine, arginine, mannose, theobromine, lysine, Na+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, and Pb2+, were prepared in a 0.5 mM concentration and added to the sampling zone of the proposed design using the same procedure of applying the samples and standards. As shown in Fig. 5 A, except for AA and Fe3+, the tested species do not exhibit any significant response towards the CD–ethidium-bromide-based probes in the solution form. Furthermore, we used NaF as a masking agent on the device to successfully mask Fe3+ and, thus, eliminate its fluorescence quenching effect on the CDs. The optimisation of the NaF concentration is presented in Fig. 5E and F. Therefore, we concluded that the proposed ratiometric μPAD combined with the 3D connector responds specifically towards AA without significant interference from other substances. Accordingly, the sensing platform is appropriate for the selective detection of AA in real serum samples after dilution. The results of the selectivity test for common cations, amino acids, and biomolecules are shown in Fig. 5B, C, and D, respectively.

To verify the suitability of the proposed method for the quantitative determination of AA in human serum samples, we further examined the device using serum samples spiked with different AA concentrations. As shown in Table 2 , four unspiked serum samples within the concentration range of 41.26–46.11 μM were each spiked with 5, 10, and 15 μM AA serum solutions and analysed, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) ranging between 1.38 and 4.51 % and recoveries ranging between 87.27 and 98.52 %. These results indicate the feasibility and reliability of the AA detection strategy in living systems.

A dual-spot optical-based ratiometric μPAD was designed, successfully compensating for instrumental and environmental variations in analytical detection. This design offers flexibility in using different camera positions, lighting conditions, and setups for the measurements. The analytical accuracy was significantly enhanced using the dual-spot mode. The proposed μPAD was functionalized with a CD–ethidium-bromide-based sensor for the selective detection of ascorbic acid. Furthermore, the proposed sensor is portable and requires reduced sample and reagent volumes, providing rapid quantitative analysis while also being simple to use. Optical detection can be conducted using only a UV dark box and a smartphone, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed paper-based sensor for the quantification of ascorbic acid.
Sabah H. Al-Jaf: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft. Khalid M. Omer: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Sabah H. Al-Jaf gratefully acknowledges the Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research of Kurdistan Regional Government as well as University of Garmian for their permission and accepting his Study Leave.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.125433.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in ratiometric luminescence sensors
Appl. Spectrosc. Rev.
